title: Make `session$destroy` preserve statically defined inputs
rationale: Facts: reporter ml-ebs-ext (DaVinci Shiny module ecosystem team) explains that after adopting session$destroy() (added in #4372, merged 2026-05-29) to clear leftover reactive state in dynamically re-rendered modules, statically-declared inputs (e.g. selectInput(ns('id'))) lose their server-side representation on destroy just like dynamically-created ones; a reactive depending on input[['id']] then errors until the user re-interacts with the widget, hurting the edit-feedback loop for point-and-click config UIs. Requests either sparing statically-defined inputs from destroy, or an opt-in flag (e.g. preserve_input_state=TRUE) to keep all input state. Classification hypothesis: feature/enhancement -- a scope refinement of the recently-shipped session$destroy() API, not a bug in the existing spec (destroy is documented/intended to remove all module reactive state). Duplicate check: searched 'session destroy preserve input' across rstudio/shiny issues (open+closed); no other match besides itself. Wrong-location check: session$destroy() lives in rstudio/shiny (added by PR #4372); correct repo. Regression check: not applicable -- behavior is exactly as implemented by #4372/#4395, not a change from older released behavior; no regression label warranted. Reproduction plan: no runnable minimal app included, but the request cites concrete API (session$destroy(), ns('id') selectInput) and a real-world integration (linked observer_dedup routine) maintainers can use to write a small module-based test demonstrating static-input loss. Impact: moderate -- affects developers building parameter-driven module UIs with re-render-on-change (DaVinci ecosystem cited); narrower than a general shiny bug since it only affects adopters of the new destroy() feature, but the ergonomic cost (visible errors after every change) is significant for that group. Priority: Medium -- well-defined, actionable enhancement request with moderate impact and a suggested workaround (avoid static inputs) already known to the reporter. Recommended next action: maintainers decide whether destroy() should default to preserving statically-declared inputs or add an opt-in flag; not risky to label and close out triage.

title: MCP Apps: tool to update an already-running app in place (auto-registered update_<appId>_app)
rationale: Facts: MEMBER schloerke opens a follow-up design issue to #4414 (merged mcpConfigure()/init-args work) proposing an auto-registered update_<appId>_app companion tool so an MCP host/model can push new arguments into an already-running Shiny MCP app's mcpUpdates() reactive channel in place, instead of only being able to re-invoke the open_* tool (which today causes hosts like Claude to render a duplicate app instance rather than update the live one). Lays out session-targeting options (explicit handle, most-recent, broadcast) and open questions on session-registry lifecycle. Classification hypothesis: feature/design-tracking issue for an experimental, still-evolving MCP Apps capability; author explicitly scopes it as 'warrants its own brainstorm/spec'. Duplicate check: searched 'MCP update running app' in rstudio/shiny issues; only this issue matches; related issues #4414 and #4407 are merged PRs/features this proposal builds on, not duplicates. Wrong-location check: MCP Apps work lives under mcp/ in rstudio/shiny; correct repo. Regression check: not applicable -- new capability proposal, no prior released behavior being compared. Reproduction plan: not applicable; design issue with open questions rather than a bug repro. Impact: low today -- MCP Apps is an experimental feature (per sibling issue #4408) not yet blocking any release; the friction described (duplicate app instances on parameter change) affects only early MCP-host integrations. Priority: Low -- internal design/tracking issue authored by a maintainer for an experimental feature, no user-facing urgency. Recommended next action: maintainers pick a session-targeting design (most-recent/broadcast recommended as default per the issue) before implementing the auto-registered update tool; not risky to close out triage.

title: [Bug]: Caret Drift BUG repeating even after updating to ver 1.6.1
rationale: Facts: originally filed May 2026 reporting that the previously-fixed (per #2223, closed 2026-04-29) caret-drift bug in ui.input_code_editor recurs on shiny 1.6.1; maintainers cpsievert and gadenbuie confirmed the repro and identified a CSS-specificity issue overriding the editor's monospace font, posting a workaround (bslib-code-editor .code-editor .prism-code-editor font-family override). New activity in this scan window is solely a 2026-07-16 comment from a second user (gafo25) confirming the same workaround fixes the issue for them on shiny 1.6.3/Python 3.13.8 -- no new technical information, just independent confirmation the underlying bug and workaround still stand on the current release. Classification hypothesis: regression (already applied by a prior triage pass on 2026-05-14, per triage-results/2026-05-14.jsonl) -- behavior regressed after the fix for #2223 landed; also already carries needs clarification since only a userland CSS override, not a permanent code fix, has shipped. Duplicate check: existing duplicate-candidate record (duplicates/candidates.jsonl line 1) already links this to closed #2223 as the earlier 'fixed' report; no new candidate to add. Wrong-location check: ui.input_code_editor/CSS lives in posit-dev/py-shiny; correct repo. Regression check: evidence already on the issue -- maintainer-confirmed CSS-specificity regression versus the intended #2223 fix; label already applied by the prior run. Reproduction plan: maintainer-provided shinylive repro already linked in the first comment; second user's confirmation reused the same repro shape. Impact: moderate -- affects any input_code_editor user on affected themes/versions; a documented CSS workaround exists, no permanent fix has shipped. Priority: unchanged from the prior pass (Priority: Medium, already present) -- still appropriate given a workaround exists and no data-loss/security angle. Recommended next action: the classification and priority labels (bug, regression, needs clarification, Priority: Medium) were already correctly applied in the 2026-05-14 pass but the completion marker was never added; adding ai-triage:done since confidence is medium and this decision adds no new labels and carries no additional risk -- maintainers should still ship the permanent CSS fix rather than relying on the userland override.
